ITEM NO.13               COURT NO.7               SECTION IX
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petitions for Special Leave to Appeal (C) Nos.23031-23032/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 07-04-2026
in AN No.118/2025 & 07-04-2026 in IA No.7621/2025 passed by the
High Court of Judicature at Bombay]
JAYA TALAKSHI CHHEDA                               Petitioner
VERSUS
TANUJA JAYANTILAL BHAGAT & ORS.                    Respondents
I.A. No.191943/2026-EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
 
Date : 17-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE DIPANKAR DATTA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SHEEL NAGU
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. A. K. Ganguli, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Prashant Padmanabhan, AOR
                   Mr. K. T. Kukreja, Adv.
                                      
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Respondent no.1 - T anuja Jayantilal Bhagat had invoked the jurisdiction of
this Court under Article 136 of the Constitution of India by filing a special leave
petition1. She had challenged an order dated 17 th December, 2018 passed by
the High Court of Judicature at Bombay2 in A.N. No.358/2017. 
2. The said special leave petition came to be disposed of by a co-ordinate
Bench vide order dated 25th February, 2019 reading as follows: 
“…………
We dispose of this special leave petition with a request to the
High Court to first take up the Criminal Appeal No.968 of 2013
for final hearing as expeditiously as possible. 
It will be open to the parties in this Special Leave Petition, to
1 Special Leave Petition (C) No.4152/2019
2 High Court
1

--- Page 127 ---
request the High Court to assign an early date for hearing of
Criminal Appeal No.968 of 2013. 
We are giving this indulgence to the parties keeping in mind the
peculiar facts of this case, as the proceedings pending between
the  parties  in  the  form  of  Suit  No.3197  of  2008  and
T estamentary Suit No.100 of 2011 in the High Court  ought to
proceed only after a final decision is rendered in the stated
criminal appeal. 
After the criminal appeal is finally disposed of, it will be open to
the parties to request the High Court to take up the civil suit
and  the  testamentary  proceedings  for  further  hearing.  The
same be decided in accordance with law expeditiously.”
(emphasis ours)
3. It  is  noted  that  the  petitioner  in  these  special  leave  petitions  -  Jaya
T alakshi  Chheda3,  was  the  respondent  no.1  in  the  aforesaid  special  leave
petition. The order, therefore, binds her.
4. Jaya had approached the Single Judge of the High Court, seized of Suit
No.3197  of  2008  and  T estamentary  Suit  No.100  of  2011,  with  prayers4 for
interim relief. The Single Judge of the High Court was disinclined to entertain the
application because of the nature of the order passed by this Court on 25 th
February, 2019. By an order dated 7 th February, 2024, liberty was granted to
Jaya to seek clarification before this Court.
5. Jaya filed a miscellaneous application 5 in the disposed of special leave
petition. She sought the following relief:  
“(a)  Clarify  that  the  Order  dated  25.02.2019 passed  by  this
Hon’ble Court in the above matter does not preclude the Hon’ble
High Court from hearing on merits and deciding the above Interim
Applications  in  the  pending  suit;  as  well  as  in  future,  if  any
Application is made.
(b) Pass such further order or orders as this Hon’ble Court may 
deem fit and proper to the facts and circumstances of the case.”
(emphasis ours)
6. The said miscellaneous application was listed before another co-ordinate
Bench of this Court. On 10th May, 2024, the following order was passed:
“ IA No. 95955/2024
3 Jaya
4 in Interim Application No.5147 of 2022
5 Miscellaneous Application No.898 of 2024
2

--- Page 128 ---
 1. We do not find any reason to clarify the order dated 25.02.2019,
as the order itself is very clear.
2. I.A. is, accordingly, dismissed.
3. M.A. is disposed of.”
7. In the backdrop of the aforesaid facts and circumstances, the Single Judge
of the High Court made an order on 29 th September, 2025, adjourning sine die
proceedings of Suit No.3197 of 2008, T estamentary Suit No.100 of 2011 and all
interlocutory applications therein, till Criminal Appeal No.698 of 2013 is finally
decided.
8. The order dated 29 th September, 2025 was then carried in appeal before
the Division Bench of the High Court by Jaya. Serious objections were raised by
the  respondents to the effect that the  proceedings cannot continue  in Suit
No.3197 of 2008 and T estamentary Suit No.100 of 2011, in view of the order
passed by this Court on 25 th February, 2019 read with the subsequent order
dated 10th May, 2024.
9. Over-ruling such objections, the Division Bench has proceeded to appoint
a receiver. Jaya does not appear to be satisfied even with this order which, to
our mind, could not have been passed having regard to the orders dated 25 th
February, 2019 and 10th May, 2024 of this Court.
10. According to Mr. Ganguly, learned senior counsel for Jaya, there was no
bar in proceeding with the pending interim applications; proceedings of the two
suits were only stayed by this Court and not proceedings relating to interim
applications.
11. We do not agree. This Court did not stop at staying the trial of the suits.
What was observed by this Court in its order dated 25 th February, 2019 is that
the “proceedings ... ought to proceed” after disposal of the criminal appeal.
Proceedings  would,  therefore,  take  within  its  fold  even  pending  interim
applications. We draw inspiration for holding so bearing in mind the fact that the
3

--- Page 129 ---
prayer made by Jaya (that there is no bar for the High Court to hear and decide
pending  interim  applications  and  applications  that  are  filed  in  future)  was
categorically refused by the order dated 10th May, 2024.   
12. In such circumstances, the impugned order dated 7 th April, 2026 granting
partial relief to Jaya itself is illegal and unsustainable. 
13. We would have been justified in setting aside the partial relief granted to
Jaya but bearing in mind the principle that a party cannot be worse off for
instituting legal proceedings, we reluctantly refrain from doing so. However,
partial relief granted to Jaya shall be subject to further orders that may be
passed in the suits after the criminal appeal is disposed of. 
14. Nonetheless, we hold that neither is Jaya entitled to any further relief nor
will  the  High  Court  entertain  any  application  in  Suit  No.3197  of  2008  and
T estamentary Suit No.100 of 2011, whether pending or filed in future, unless
and until final disposal of the criminal appeal. 
15. The special leave petitions are dismissed on the aforesaid terms. 
16. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
17. We cannot help making a parting observation/direction. 
18. The approach of the Division Bench, to overrule the objections raised and
to proceed with the hearing despite noticing that the criminal appeal is yet to
be disposed of although seven years have passed in the interregnum since this
Court had made the relevant order, has surprised us without end. 
19. Be that as it may, having regard to the drastic consequences that the
order dated 25th February, 2019 read with the order dated 10 th May, 2024 could
give rise owing to stalling of the proceedings of the suits, we now require the
High Court to dispose of Criminal Appeal No.968 of 2013 at the earliest.
20. Registry is directed to forthwith forward this order to the Registrar General
4

--- Page 130 ---
of  the  High  Court  for  being  placed  before  the  Chief  Justice  to  secure  its
compliance, in letter and spirit. We deliberately do not set a timeline for such
disposal in the hope and trust that the Bench, to which the criminal appeal is
assigned by the Chief Justice, would leave no stone unturned to give it the
priority the same demands.  
      (MANIK KUMAR)                         (SUDHIR KUMAR SHARMA)
SENIOR PERSONAL ASSISTANT                     COURT MASTER (NSH)
5

--- Page 131 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.      OF 2026
[Arising out of SLP(C) No.16885/2026]
MUKESH VISHANDAYAL SHARMA & ORS. APPELLANT(S)
                              VERSUS
THE REGISTRAR GENERAL, HIGH COURT OF BOMBAY &
ORS.
RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. There are 11 appellants before us challenging the
judgment and order dated 17.02.2026 passed by the High
Court of Judicature at Bombay, whereby the appellants
have  been  held  ineligible  to  apply  for  direct
recruitment to the post of District Judge.
3. It  is  not  in  dispute  that  the  appellants  were
practicing advocates and each one of them had more than
10  years  of  experience  at  the  Bar  before  their
appointment in the District Consumer Disputes Redressal
Commission  (DCDRC)/State  Consumer  Disputes  Redressal
Commission (SCDRC). It is also an admitted fact that
appellants were/are Members of the DCDRC and SCDRC at
the time when the High Court issued the advertisement
inviting  applications  for  direct  recruitment  to  the
posts of District Judge. However, the appellants were
not permitted to apply in the category of judicial
officers on the grounds that members of the DCDRC/SCDRC
1

--- Page 132 ---
are not judicial officers. 
4. The aggrieved appellants therefore approached the
High  Court  on  the  judicial  side,  but  their  writ
petitions were dismissed, prompting them to file the
instant appeal.
5. Vide an  interim order  dated  08.05.2026,  the
appellants were permitted to appear in the preliminary
examination provisionally, subject to outcome of these
proceedings.  Pursuant  thereto,  the  appellants  were
permitted to appear in the preliminary examination and
out of 11, 3 of them, namely, Mukesh Vishandayal Sharma,
Mandakini  Bhosale  and  Harish  Govind  Adake,  have
qualified. 
6. Adverting to the main issue, namely, eligibility of
the appellants, we are of the view that since each of
them had completed more than 10 years practice at the
Bar,  before  their  appointment  as  members  of  the
DCDRC/SCDRC, they cannot be denied eligibility to apply
to the posts of District Judge merely because they were
not in actual practice on the cut-off date given by the
Competent Authority in the subject-advertisement.
7. Consequently,  in  the  peculiar  facts  and
circumstances of this case, it seems to us that the
appellants were entitled to be treated as eligible under
the category of Advocates, as they had practiced for
more than 10 years before becoming member(s) of the
DCDRC/SCDRC. 
2

--- Page 133 ---
8. Accordingly,  the  instant  appeal  is  allowed.  The
three  appellants  who  have  qualified  the  preliminary
examination  are  permitted  to  appear  in  the  main
examination as well. The necessary consequences shall
follow. 
9. Needless to say, if the three qualified appellants
are  declared  eligible  in  the  examination(s),  their
candidature shall further be considered in accordance
with the relevant Rules.
10. It is, however, clarified that this order shall not
be treated as a precedent by any other candidates to
claim eligibility.
..........................CJI.
(SURYA KANT)
............................J.
(JOYMALYA BAGCHI) 
............................J.
(V. MOHANA) 
       
NEW DELHI;        
JULY 16, 2026
3

--- Page 134 ---

1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant challenges the judgment and order dated
10.02.2026 in Misc. Criminal Case No.6608/2026 passed by
the High Court of Madhya Pradesh at Gwalior.
3. On  24.03.2026,  this  Court  passed  the  following
order:-
“1.  Petitioner  challenges  the  judgment
and  order  dated  10.02.2026  in  MCRC
No.6608/2026 passed by the High Court of
Madhya Pradesh at Gwalior, titled “Hari
Singh vs. The State of Madhya Pradesh”. 
2. We have heard learned counsel for the
petitioner.
3.  Issue  notice,  returnable  on
30.04.2026.
4.  Mr.  Harmeet  Singh  Ruprah,  learned
counsel, accepts notice on behalf of the
respondent/State.  Hence,  the  formal
service of notice on the respondent is
waived.
5. Learned counsel for the petitioner to
serve  an  advance  copy  of  the  present
petition to the learned counsel for the
respondent within a period of one (01)
week.

1

--- Page 137 ---
three  (03)  weeks.  Rejoinder  affidavit
thereto, if any, be filed before the next
date of listing.

purpose.
8. In the event of arrest in connection
with  Crime  No.269  of  2023  of  Basoda
Police Station, Dehat District, Vidisha,
Madhya Pradesh the petitioner shall be

and conditions as may be fixed by the

available  before  the  Investigating
Officer on 06.04.2026 at 10:00 a.m. and
on all such dates as he may be required.
10.  Needless  to  add,  till  the
investigation  is  not  complete  in  all
respects,  the  petitioner  would  fully
cooperate,  and  if  the  challan/charge
sheet is filed, he would maintain good
conduct and not attempt to influence any
of the witnesses in any manner till the
completion of the trial.”
4. We have heard learned counsel for the parties and
also perused the materials available on record.
5. It is not in dispute that the conditions stand fully
complied with; the appellant has cooperated during the
investigation; he has not threatened or intimidated any
of  the  witnesses;  or  has  tried  to  influence  the
investigation in any manner.
6. We have noticed the nature of crime and the manner in
which it was allegedly committed.
7. We allow the present appeal by confirming the order
dated 24.03.2026, passed by this Court, with a direction
to the appellant to continue to cooperate during the

--- Page 138 ---
investigation/trial  and  not  to  take  any  unnecessary

Investigating Officer every alternate Monday, between 10
a.m. and 11 a.m., till such time the investigation is
complete in all respects.
8. If the Investigating Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial
Court feels that the investigation/trial is delayed on
account of the appellant’s conduct, it shall be open to
the Investigating Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial Court
to take appropriate steps in accordance with the law,
including cancellation of bail.
9. The impugned judgment and order dated 10.02.2026 is
set aside.
10. It is brought to our notice that now disciplinary
proceedings stand initiated against the erring officer(s)
and the show cause notice also stands issued to the
doctor(s) who issued MLC. We direct such action to be
taken to its logical end at the earliest and definitely
within a period of six (06) months from today.
11. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) No.1977/2025
SAKSHI DUBEY              PETITIONER
VERSUS
MAYANK GAUR & ANR.                         RESPONDENTS
O R D E R
1. In  course  of  hearing  of  this  transfer  petition,  the
petitioner-wife (Sakshi Dubey) and the respondent no.1-husband
(Mayank Gaur) were referred to the Supreme Court Mediation
Centre.
2. The process of mediation has produced positive results.
The Mediator has sent a report that the parties have agreed to
resolve the inter se disputes and differences amicably. It has also
been  decided  by  them  to  apply  before  this  Court  seeking
dissolution of their marriage by mutual consent under Article 142
1

--- Page 142 ---
of the Constitution of India.
3. The parties have jointly filed an application under Article
142 of the Constitution of India in Court, which is taken on record.
The prayers in the application under Article 142 read as follows:
“a) Pass  an order taking the Settlement agreement
dated  02.07.2026  (Annexure  A-1)  between  the
parties (the Petitioner and Respondent No.1) on
record and pass appropriate orders in terms of the
said agreement;
b) Pass  an  order  dissolving  the  marriage  dated
12.02.2023 between the Petitioner Sakshi Dubey
and Respondent No.1 Mayank Gaur, on grounds of
irretrievable breakdown of marriage and/or;
c)  direct  quashing  of  all  proceedings/cases  as
mentioned in para 7 of  this application (or  any
other  civil/criminal/matrimonial  found  to  be
subsequently pending between the parties or their
relatives /friends) before the relevant competent
courts of law; and/or
d)  direct that henceforth no party shall file any case
arising  out  of  this  marriage  and  shall  live
peacefully  in  the  future  without  any  acrimony;
and/or
e) pass any such order/orders as this Hon'ble Court
may deem fit in the facts and circumstances of the
case.
 
4. During the course of the dispute, the parties filed various
cases against each other. List of cases pending by and between
the parties before various courts is as under:
i) FIR No. 325 registered on 02.12.2024 at PS Sector
40, Gurugram (Case no CHI 4121/2025 titled State of
Haryana  vs  Mayank  Gaur)  under  Sections  323, 406
and 498A of the IPC, pending before the Ld. JMFC,
Gururgram District Court at Gurugram.
ii) HMA No. 431 of 2025 titled Mayank Gaur Vs Sakshi
2

--- Page 143 ---
Dubey pending before the Additional Principal Judge,
Family Court, Gurugram.
iii) Petition-A No. 979 of 2025 titled Sakshi Dubey vs
Mayank  Gaur  pending  before  the  Family  Court  at
Bandra, Mumbai.
5. We have interacted with the petitioner and the respondent
no.1 who have joined the proceedings virtually today. They have
separately submitted, while responding to our query, that they
have agreed to part ways without any threat, force, coercion or
undue  influence.  We  record  their  further  statement  that  the
mutual obligations as per the settlement dated 2 nd July, 2026
have been worked out.
6. The parties having amicably resolved the inter se disputes
and differences, the terms of settlement contained in the joint
application shall form part of this order.
7. Accordingly,  in  exercise  of  power  conferred  on  us  by
Article 142 of the Constitution of India, we dissolve the marriage
between the parties by mutual consent by granting orders in
terms of prayer (b) of the joint application as well as prayers (c)
& (d) thereof. Decree be drawn up, accordingly.
8. As a result of this order, nothing survives for decision on
the transfer petition. The transfer petition stands disposed of. 
9. The relevant courts/police stations shall mark closure of
the  cases/proceedings/complaints  that  are  pending/lodged
before it, once any party approaches it with a copy of this order.
3

--- Page 144 ---
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.
11. We appreciate the efforts of the mediator in assisting the
parties to resolve their disputes amicably.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.3233 OF 2026
(@SLP(CRL.) NO.15595 OF 2025)
SUMAN KUMAR SINHA ..... APPELLANT(S)
              VERSUS
STATE OF JHARKHAND         ..... RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Heard.
2. Leave granted.
3. The wife of the appellant is said to have expired on
13.02.2022 due to burn injuries and her death occurred in the
matrimonial  home  within  seven  years  of  the  marriage.
Undisputedly, the mother of appellant was present at the
matrimonial home and she has also been arraigned as an
accused  in  the  FIR  No.22/2022,  which  resulted  in
investigation being taken up resulted in chargesheet being
filed for offences punishable under Section 304B read with
Section 34 of the Indian Penal Code against appellant’s
1                                                                                     SLP(CRL.) NO.15595 OF 2025

--- Page 147 ---
mother  (Accused  No.2).  It  was  also  recorded  that  the
appellant was located in Maharashtra and allegation against
him was unfounded. That apart, the eye witnesses stated that
the incident was accidental. On a negative report being filed
by the jurisdictional police, the complainant, namely, the
mother of the deceased, lodged a protest petition before the
jurisdictional Magistrate, which resulted in cognizance being
taken and the prayer for anticipatory bail of appellant being
rejected. The High Court, by the impugned order, has also
rejected the prayer of the appellant. Hence, this appeal.
4.  This  Court  while  issuing  notice  vide  order  dated
22.09.2025  granted  interim  protection  to  the  appellant.
Having regard to the aforestated facts and there was a
negative report filed by the jurisdictional Investigating
Officer (IO) and also taking into consideration that the
child of the appellant, who is aged seven years, is living
with the appellant, we are of the view that appellant if
taken into custody, the child would be left in the lurch and
in the circumstances narrated hereinabove, we are of the
considered  view  that  his  custodial  interrogation,
particularly, in the backdrop of chargesheet having been
filed, would not be warranted.
2                                                                                     SLP(CRL.) NO.15595 OF 2025

--- Page 148 ---
5. Accordingly, appeal is allowed. Impugned order is set
aside. Appellant is ordered to be released on anticipatory
bail on such terms and conditions as the jurisdictional IO
may deem fit to impose and also on the condition of appellant
appearing before the IO as and when called upon for further
investigation, if any, and appellant shall also appear before
the jurisdictional court on all dates of hearing except when
exempted.
6. It is also made clear that appellant shall deposit his
passport, if any, and intimate to the IO of his whereabouts
every week by forwarding the mail or communication to the
jurisdictional IO. 
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
..................J.
(ARAVIND KUMAR)
..................J.
(VIPUL M. PANCHOLI)
New Delhi;
July 17, 2026.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO.1509 OF 2026
 PUMMY KUMARI                                       PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
 AMRITESH KUMAR                                     RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. The transfer petition has been filed seeking transfer of the
proceedings, bearing HMA No.1450 of 2025 under Section 13(i) (ia)
of the Hindu Marriage Act, titled “Amritesh Kumar Vs. Pummy Kumari”
pending before the learned Principal Judge, Family Court, South
East, Saket to the learned Presiding Officer, Family Court at
District Darbhanga, Bihar.
2. Heard learned counsel appearing for both the parties.
3. Learned counsel for the Petitioner submits that the marriage
between the parties was solemnized on 18.01.2021 and they have been
living separately since 2025. The Petitioner/wife is a permanent
resident of Darbhanga, Bihar and presently residing there with her
parents.
4. Learned counsel for the Petitioner further submits that due to
certain dispute between the parties, an FIR bearing No.109 of 2025
dated  07.10.2025  was  registered  at  Sakatpur  Police  Station,
Darbhanga under Sections 126(2), 115(2), 117(2), 85, 352, 351(2) of
the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 and Section 3(iv) of the Dowry
Prohibition Act against the Respondent/husband and his family
1

--- Page 152 ---
members. Subsequently, the Respondent/husband has filed a petition
before the learned Principal Judge, Family Court, South East,
Saket.
5. It is also submitted that the Petitioner is unemployed and she
has no person to accompany her in the journey from Darbhanga to
Delhi. She has already initiated criminal proceedings against the
Respondent and that the divorce petition has been filed only to
harass her and cause mental torture and physical hardship.
6. Learned  counsel  appearing  for  the  Respondent,  under
instructions, submits that though it is a fact that the Petitioner
is residing at Darbhanga, Bihar, she is highly educated and she is
in a position to take care of herself and fully capable of
appearing before the Court.
7. It is also submitted by the Respondent that the Petitioner’s
mother belongs to Delhi.
8. However, having regard to the fact that there is already a
criminal case pending in Dharbhanga, Bihar and that the Petitioner
is residing there permanently with her parents, it is just and
proper to transfer the case.
9. Consequently,  it  is  considered  appropriate  that  petition
bearing HMA No.1450 of 2025 under Section 13(i) (ia) of the Hindu
Marriage Act, titled “ Amritesh Kumar Vs. Pummy Kumari ” pending
before the learned Principal Judge, Family Court, South East,
Saket, is transferred to the learned Presiding Officer, Family
Court at District Darbhanga, Bihar.
10. In case either party shall request for appearance through
Video Conferencing (VC) mode, the transferee Court may consider it
2

--- Page 153 ---
and pass appropriate directions in accordance with law.  
11. The Transfer Petition stands allowed in the aforesaid terms.
12. Records shall be sent by the Court where proceedings are
pending to the Transferee Court promptly and without any delay.
13. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
.......................J.
                                          (V. MOHANA)
NEW DELHI;
JULY 17, 2026
3

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA 
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION 
CRIMINAL APPEAL No.         OF 2026
                  [arising out of SLP (Crl.) No. 6078 OF 2026]            
 
ABHIMANYU KUMAR @ LALU                             APPELLANT
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF BIHAR                                 RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The  High  Court  of  Judicature  at  Patna,  by  the  impugned
judgment  and  order  dated  25th February,  2026,  has  rejected  the
appellant’s application for regular bail.
3. Appellant was arrested on 19 th April, 2025 in connection with
FIR No.57 of 2025 dated 18 th April, 2024 registered at Police Station
Motihari  Cyber  P .S.  under  Sections  338,  336(2),  340(2),  303(2),
318(4), 319(2) and 317(5) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 and
Sections 66C and 66D of the Information T echnology Act, 2000.
4. We have heard learned counsel appearing for the parties and
perused the materials on record.
5. Citing grant of bail to six co-accused, parity is claimed. 

--- Page 156 ---
2
6. The police report (charge-sheet) has been filed on 14 th July,
2025 but the charges have yet not been framed. T rial is, therefore,
likely to take time to conclude.
7. T aking an overall view of the matter, we are of the considered
opinion  that  the  appellant  need  not  be  detained  in  custody  any
longer and that he could be admitted to an order for release on bail
pending trial.
8. Accordingly, we set aside the impugned judgment and order.
9. Appellant be released on bail subject to producing bail bonds to
the satisfaction of the trial court and subject also to such other terms
and conditions that are imposed by it.
10. Needless  to  observe,  the  appellant  shall  not,  directly  or
indirectly, by making inducement, threat or promise, dissuade any
person acquainted with the facts of the case from disclosing such
facts to the court.
11. In the event there is any breach of the terms and conditions for
grant of bail, the trial court shall be at liberty to cancel the bail of the
appellant.
12. It  is  also  ordered  that  the  appellant  shall  diligently  attend
proceedings  of  the  trial,  unless  exempted.  If  he  abstains  from
attending the proceedings without justifiable cause, that could also
be seen as breach of the conditions for grant of bail and the trial
court will be free to pass appropriate orders.

--- Page 157 ---
3
13. We clarify that the observations made in this order and grant of
bail will not be treated as findings on the merits of the case.
14.  The appeal is, accordingly, allowed on the aforesaid terms.
15.  Pending  interlocutory application(s), if any, stand disposed of.
…………….............................J.
[DIPANKAR DATTA]
……………..............................J.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION   (Civil) No. 853 OF 2026  
PRIYANKA THAKUR                                    PETITIONER
                                VERSUS
NIRAV JAWAHARSINH THAKUR                        RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. Read the office report dated 15th July, 2026.
2. This  transfer petition,  under Section  25 of the  Code  of Civil
Procedure, 1908, has been presented by the petitioner – Priyanka
Thakur.
3. The marriage between the petitioner and the respondent – Nirav
Jawaharsinh Thakur, was solemnized on 11 th June, 2023 as per Hindu
rites and customs.  However, the parties have since drifted apart
owing to differences arising by and between them.
4. The respondent has instituted proceedings under Section 13 of
the  Hindu  Marriage  Act,  1955,  bearing  F.S.  No.  2942/2024  titled
“Nirav Jawaharsinh Thakur vs. Priyanka Thakur ” in the Family Court,
Ahmedabad, Gujarat.
5. On the multiple grounds urged in the petition, the petitioner has
sought for transfer of the aforesaid proceedings to the Family Court
at Sagar, Madhya Pradesh.
6. Despite service, none has entered appearance on behalf of the

--- Page 164 ---
2
respondent-husband.   We are, thus, inclined to the view that the
respondent  has  no  serious  objection  to  grant  of  the  prayer  for
transfer.
7. We  are  also  otherwise  satisfied,  having  regard  to  the
inconvenience the petitioner is likely to face if the proceedings were
continued in  the Family Court, Ahmedabad, Gujarat,  which is at a
distance of around 723 kms from her present place of residence,
that interest of justice demands grant of the prayer for transfer.
8. Hence, the transfer petition is allowed. F.S. No. 2942 of 2024
titled  “Nirav  Jawaharsinh  Thakur  vs.  Priyanka  Thakur”  pending
before the Family Court, Ahmedabad, Gujarat is transferred  to the
Family Court at Sagar, Madhya Pradesh.  Records of F.S. No. 2942 of
2024 be transferred to the transferee court immediately.
9. To avoid delay, the parties are directed to appear before the
transferee court on 17.08.2026. If any or both the parties fail to
appear on that day, the presiding officer of the transferee court will
fix another date of appearance. Thereafter, he/she shall be free to
proceed in accordance with law.
10. The  presiding  officer  shall  endeavour  to  bring  about  a
settlement between the parties through the process of mediation. If
the  process  is  unsuccessful,  nothing  shall  preclude  the  presiding
officer to take the proceedings to its logical conclusion on its merits
and in accordance with law.
11. If the respondent is unable to attend the proceedings in person
on any occasion, he will be at liberty to seek permission to attend

--- Page 165 ---
3
through the virtual mode and if such a permission is sought, the
presiding officer of the court may proceed to consider it reasonably.
12. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
…………….............................J.
[DIPANKAR DATTA]
……………..............................J.
[SHEEL  NAGU]
  New Delhi;
July 16, 2026.

      IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA

VERSUS
RAVI KUMAR    RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. Read the office report dated 15th July, 2026.
2. Mediation report dated 4 th June, 2026 has been received from

--- Page 187 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.           OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP (Crl.) No. 3084 of 2026)
JAGAT RAM                          APPELLANT(S)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF HIMACHAL PRADESH & ANR.             RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant challenges the judgment and final order
dated 09.10.2025 passed by the High Court of Himachal Pradesh
at Shimla in Criminal Appeal No. 459 of 2010 ( Jagat Ram vs.
State of H.P.), whereby the conviction and sentence imposed by
the Trial Court vide judgment dated 30.09.2010 in Sessions
Trial No. 5/S7 of 2008 was affirmed.
3. As per the office report, Respondent No. 2 (complainant,
Puran  Chand)  stands  served  but  has  chosen  not  to  enter
appearance.
4. The appellant stands convicted under Section 324 of the
Indian Penal Code (IPC), 1860, and was sentenced to undergo
simple imprisonment for two years with a fine of 2,000/-, and₹
in default of payment, further imprisonment. The conviction
arises out of FIR No. 33/2007 dated 02.02.2007 registered at
Police Station Dhalli, District Shimla.
1

--- Page 188 ---
5. The prosecution case is that on 02.02.2007, complainant
Puran Chand (PW-12), accompanied by his wife Padma Devi (PW-
13), was attacked by the appellant with a sharp-edged weapon
(darat), causing an incised injury on his shoulder. The Trial
Court acquitted the appellant of offences under Sections 307
and 506 IPC but convicted him under Section 324 IPC, which was
subsequently affirmed by the High Court.
6. We have heard learned counsel for the parties. On the
issue of conviction, we find no merit for interference. We had
already expressed our opinion vide order dated 27.02.2026 that
the High Court’s affirmation of the conviction requires no
further consideration.
7. However, on the question of the quantum of sentence, we
are inclined to accept the appellant's prayer for a liberal
view. The incident relates back to the year 2007, nearly two
decades ago. The appellant has faced the agony of criminal
proceedings  for  a  significant  duration,  and  the  record
indicates  he  has  maintained  good  conduct  during  the
investigation, trial, and the pendency of appeals.
8. Considering the nature of the offence—involving a single
blow without established prior enmity—and the substantial time
lag, we are of the view that the custodial sentence of two
years is disproportionate to the gravity and circumstances of
the case.
9. Accordingly, we modify the sentence imposed by the Courts
below. The sentence of imprisonment is converted into the
payment of a fine of 1,00,000/- (Rupees One Lakh only). Out of₹
2

--- Page 189 ---
this total amount, a sum of 75,000/- (Rupees Seventy-Five₹
Thousand  only)  shall  be  paid  as  compensation  to  the
complainant, Puran Chand (Respondent No. 2).
10. The  total  amount  of  1,00,000/-  shall  be  positively₹
deposited/paid within a period of eight weeks from today. In
case of default in such deposit or payment, without any further
reference to this Court, the original sentence of two years
simple imprisonment as imposed by the Trial Court and affirmed
by the High Court shall revive automatically.
11. With the above modification, the appeal stands disposed
of.
12. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed 
 .....................J. 
          (SANJAY KAROL)    
.........................J. 
                                    (AUGUSTINE GEORGE MASIH)    
NEW DELHI;
JULY 13, 2026
3

--- Page 190 ---

CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION

NIKITA VINOD TIRMALI                               Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
VINOD PRAKASH TIRMALI                              Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. This is a petition filed under Section 25 of the Code of
Civil Procedure, 1908. The petitioner has filed this Transfer
Petition seeking the following reliefs:
“Transfer the proceedings bearing HMP No. 98/2025,
titled as “Vinod Prakash Tirmali vs. Nikita Vinod
Tirmali”, pending before the Civil Judge, Senior
Division at Chalisgaon, Maharashtra to the Family
Court, Surat, Gujarat”.
2.  As per the office report, respondent has been served. But
none has entered appearance on his behalf. Hence, the respondent is
proceeded ex-parte. 
3. In the result, the Transfer Petition is allowed and HMP No.
98/2025,  titled  as  “Vinod  Prakash  Tirmali  vs.  Nikita  Vinod
Tirmali”, pending before the Civil Judge, Senior Division at
Chalisgaon, Maharashtra is hereby transferred to the Family Court,
Surat, Gujarat.
4. Registry to communicate this order to the transferor court
for taking steps in accordance with this order.
1

--- Page 192 ---
5. On an application being made by the respondent, the Court
to which the case is transferred shall permit the respondent to
appear through video-conference on all dates except on the dates on
which his personal presence is absolutely mandatory.
6. The transfer petition stands allowed in the aforesaid
terms.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
   CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.      OF 2026
   (Arising out of SLP(Crl.) No.9129 of 2026)
    
KANARAM @ KANHAIYA LAL GURJAR                      APPELLANT
                                VERSUS
  
STATE OF RAJASTHAN & ANR.                              RESPONDENTS
R1 : STATE OF RAJASTHAN
R2 : MARTAND PRATAP SINGH
O R D E R
Heard learned counsels for the parties.
2. Leave granted.
3. At the outset, learned counsel for the respondent no.1-State
of Rajasthan submitted that due to the impugned order passed by the
High Court granting interim protection to the respondent no.2, the
State is unable to carry out further investigation. It was further
submitted that now subsequent charge sheet has also been filed
against respondent no.2.
4. Having regard to the aforesaid and going through the impugned
order, we find that the order impugned needs to be interfered with.
5. Accordingly, the interim protection granted by the High Court
of Judicature for Rajasthan at Jaipur vide impugned order dated
04.02.2025 in S.B. Criminal Miscellaneous (Petition) No.637 of 2025
stands vacated. It shall be open to the investigating agency to

--- Page 196 ---
2
proceed with the matter and take steps as may be deemed necessary
in accordance with law, as per the requirement.
6. Accordingly, the appeal stands allowed. The impugned order
dated 04.02.2025 passed by the High Court of Judicature for
Rajasthan at Jaipur in S.B. Criminal Miscellaneous (Petition)
No.637 of 2025 stands set aside.
7. Though, we feel that the impugned order passed by the High
Court requires to be taken note of seriously, but we refrain from
saying much about it; however, indicating that such type of order
ought not to have been passed by the High Court in view of the
nature of the proceedings in which it has been done.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
          ……………………..………………………………………………J.
[AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH]
…………………………………………………………………………J.
                   [MANMOHAN]
NEW DELHI
JULY 16, 2026

                   Dr. Abhishek Atrey, Adv.

 UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                        O R D E R
The  directions  issued  by  the  Court  are
reproduced  hereunder :
9. Having  considered  the  submissions
advanced at bar and after going through the
material placed on record, particularly the
Google images shown to the Court by the
learned counsel for the appellants during
2

--- Page 213 ---
the course of hearing, we are of the opinion
that  a  detailed  survey  of  the  area  in
question  has  to  be  carried  out  by  an
independent oversight agency.  In this view
of the matter, we hereby direct the Central
Empowered Committee (CEC), constituted by
this  Court  [now  functioning  under  the
Environment  (Protection)  Act,  1986],  to
nominate a competent official for carrying
out an extensive survey of the area in
question and to submit a detailed report for
the perusal of this Court.  While conducting
the survey/inspection, the CEC shall also
examine the issue as to whether the project
in  question  is  permissible  without
environmental  clearances  and  without
approval  of  building  plans  from  the
concerned local authority. The CEC shall
also examine the issue of felling of trees
and destruction of forest cover, if any, and
encroachment  on  Government  lands  during
construction of the approach roads to the
site in question. The comparative status of
the tree cover in the subject area during
the last 10 years shall be kept in mind by
the CEC while making the inspection.  The
report shall be furnished to this Court in a
sealed cover within three weeks.
10. Learned AOR for the appellants and
the respondents shall contact and provide
all necessary documents/details to the CEC
for conducting this inspection/survey.  The
concerned  officials  of  the  Forest  and
Revenue  Departments  of  the  State  of
Uttarakhand along with the Police Department
3

--- Page 214 ---
and  the  District  Administration  shall
cooperate with and provide all requisite
facilities to the CEC/its authorized officer
for carrying out the inspection.
11. The CEC shall indicate to this Court
the expenditure borne in carrying out the
aforesaid exercise.  The appellants as well
as the private respondents shall bear the
aforesaid expenditure in the ratio of 50:50.
12. Registry is directed to communicate a
copy of this order to the Central Empowered
Committee (CEC) forthwith. “
List  on 9th September, 2026 in terms of the signed
order which is placed on the file.
(SONIA BHASIN)

(RANJANA SHAILEY)

  UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
In Criminal Appeal No(s). 1278/2023
1. It is argued that the main accused Jai Deo Singh has
already been granted pardon by the State Government; and
the appellant – Shailendra Singh, aged 77 years has
already suffered more than nine years of incarceration
and moreover, he is partially blind and unable to walk
properly. 
2. Considering the entire facts and situation of the
case,  we  direct  the  appellant  -  Shailendra  Singh’s
release on bail subject to such terms and conditions as
may be imposed by the Trial Court.
3. Accordingly, IA No. 88270/2026 is allowed.
In Criminal Appeal No(s). 1431/2019
1. Appellant Narendra Singh is aged about 76 years and
is  in  jail  since  more  than  nine  years  including
remission, therefore, considering that the main accused
has already been granted pardon by Hon'ble Governor of
the State concerned, we direct appellant - Narendra
Singh's release on bail on such terms and conditions as
may be imposed by the Trial Court.
2. Accordingly, IA No. 88411/2026 is allowed.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.         /2026
[Arising out of SLP(C) No.23016/2026]
V ARASAYEE AMMAL & ANR.              APPELLANTS
VERSUS
SELVARAJ & ORS.            RESPONDENTS
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The impugned judgment and order dated 26 th February, 2026 passed by
the High Court of Judicature at Madras at Madurai 1 allowing C.R.P . (MD) No.78 of
20262, reverses the order of the First Appellate Court, condoning the delay of
263 days in filing of an appeal under Section 96 of the Code of Civil Procedure,
1908  by  the  judgment-debtor/appellant,  and  dismisses  the  appeal  as  time-
barred,  on  the  ground  that  the  First  Appellate  Court  did  not  look  into  the
counter-affidavit  and  additional  counter-affidavit  filed  by  the  decree
holder/respondent and proceeded to decide such application for condonation
based on oral arguments only.
3. This finding of the learned Judge of the High Court is clearly contrary to
the order dated 31 st July, 2024 of the First Appellate Court made in course of
condoning the delay.
1 High Court
2 revisional application
1

--- Page 220 ---
4. Discussion under Header Nos.4 and 5 of the order dated 31 st July, 2024
clearly  evince  that  the  counter-affidavit  and  the  additional  counter-affidavit
were duly considered. 
5. Non-application of mind being writ large, we have no other option but to
set aside the impugned judgment and order and to order a remand. Ordered
accordingly. 
6. This would result in revival of the revisional application before the High
Court.  The  High  Court  shall  decide  the  revisional  application  of  the  decree
holder-respondent in accordance with law as early as possible, preferably within
four months from date of service of a copy of this order on the Registry of the
High Court.
7. All points and contentions on merit are kept open.
8. Till the revisional application is disposed of, execution proceedings 3 shall
remain stayed.
9. The civil appeal is, accordingly, allowed on the above terms.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
...........................................J.
(DIPANKAR DATTA)
………...................................J.
(SHEEL NAGU)
 New Delhi;
July 17, 2026.
3 E.P . No.13 of 2024

lakh eighty thousand) under this order.

further,  the  respondent  herein  will  deposit  unpaid

explored through the process of mediation.

LOKESH BHATIA                                      RESPONDENT(S)
FOR ADMISSION 
IA No. 192242/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
IA No. 192241/2026 - STAY APPLICATION
 
Date : 14-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SANJAY KAROL
For Petitioner(s) : Ms. Shalini Jha, Adv.
     Mr. Binay Kumar Das, AOR
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
Upon hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                               O R D E R
1. The present Transfer Petition has been filed seeking
the transfer of proceedings, being DMC/117/2026, titled
“Lokesh Bhatia Vs. Ritika”, pending before the Court of the
learned Principal Judge, Family Court, Sonipat, Haryana to
the Court of the learned Principal Judge, Family Court,
Kotdwar, District Pauri-Garhwal, Uttarakhand.
2. On the first call, the matter was taken up. None entered
appearance on behalf of the petitioner. Hence, the matter
was passed over. Even on the second call, after the pass
over, when none appeared, this Court, in the interest of
1

--- Page 247 ---
justice,  appointed  Ms.  Vritti  Jain  (Email  ID:
vrittiijainn@gmail.com and  Enrolment  No.D/9261/2025),  a
young counsel who is present in Court, as an amicus curiae
(pro bono) to assist the Court on behalf of the petitioner.
To enable the learned amicus curiae to read the Court file
and assist the Court, the matter was again passed over. On
the third call, Ms. Shalini Jha, learned counsel, entered
appearance  on  behalf  of  the  petitioner.  However,  the
learned amicus curiae is permitted to make her submissions.
3. Heard the submissions advanced by the learned amicus
curiae.
4. Since the dispute primarily arises out of matrimonial
discord, this Court would implore the parties to endeavour
to reach an amicable resolution of the dispute, which would
not only ensure finality and certainty of the relationship
but also resolve the several proceedings pending inter se
the parties.
5. Let notice be issued, returnable on 03.09.2026.
6. Dasti service, in addition, is permitted. Let steps for
service be taken within two (02) weeks.
7. In  the  notice  itself,  let  it  be  mentioned  that
respondent is required to file the counter affidavit and
reply to the interlocutory application(s), if any, before
the next date of listing.
8. In the meanwhile, all further proceedings inter se the
parties arising out of matrimonial discord shall remain
stayed.
2

--- Page 248 ---
9. However, if there is any order of maintenance passed by
any appropriate authority, the same shall continue to be
complied with by the respondent-husband.
10. It is clarified that the learned amicus curiae shall
continue to assist the Court even if the petitioner is
represented by a duly engaged counsel.
11. The Registry shall print the name of the learned amicus
curiae in the cause list.
12. The Registry is directed to supply the complete paper
book,  in  digital  form,  to  the  learned  amicus  curiae
forthwith.
  (D. NAVEEN)                        (ANU BHALLA)
        COURT MASTER (SH)                 COURT MASTER (NSH)

ITEM NO.33               COURT NO.7               SECTION IX
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal(C) No(s). 12610/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 04-03-2026
in IA No. 1671/2026 passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Bombay]
VINOD MANGALDAS SHAH & ORS.                        Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
IFFCO TOKIO GENERAL INSURANCE CO. LTD. & ORS.      Respondent(s)
IA No. 104503/2026 - ADDITION / DELETION / MODIFICATION  PARTIES
IA No. 104502/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
IA No. 104504/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 17-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE DIPANKAR DATTA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SHEEL NAGU
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Ravindra Keshavrao Adsure, AOR
                   Mr. Sanjay Ashok Raut, Adv.
                   Mr. Yash Prashant Sonavane, Adv.
                   Ms. Vishwabharati Vitthal Devkhile, Adv.
                                      
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Petitioners were the claimants before the relevant Motor Accident Claims
Tribunal1. They are the heirs and dependents of the deceased victim. Their
claim under Section 166 of the Motor Vehicles Act, 19882 succeeded.
2. In  terms  of  the  award  of  the  tribunal,  allegedly,  they  are  entitled  to
Rs.19,20,24,467/- [Rupees nineteen crore  twenty lakh twenty four thousand
1 tribunal
2 ACT

--- Page 253 ---
2
four hundred and sixty seven] only, inclusive of interest.
3. IFFCO-TOKIO General Insurance Company Limited 3, being the insurer of
the offending vehicle, challenged the award in an appeal 4 before the High Court
under Section 173 of the Act. The insurance company also applied for a stay of
the execution proceedings.
4. Submission of the insurance company was recorded by the Division Bench
of the High Court in the impugned order dated 4 th March, 2026 that it is not
challenging  the  quantum  of  compensation  awarded  by  the  tribunal  to  the
claimants; the challenge is confined to the findings recorded by the tribunal
attributing  contributory  negligence  to  the  driver  of  the  vehicle  which  was
insured with the insurance company.
5. Having regard to the fact that the insurance company has mounted a
limited challenge to the award and no challenge was laid to the quantum of
compensation awarded by the tribunal in favour of the petitioners, we would
have expected the High Court to direct satisfaction of the award by ensuring full
payment of  the  amount  awarded  with  the  observation  that, subject  to  the
insurance company succeeding in the appeal, it would be entitled to recover
from the party ultimately found liable any amount paid by it in excess of its
liability. Instead, what we find is that the execution proceedings have been
stayed by the High Court by the impugned order subject to payment of only
1/4th of the awarded amount without any security.
6. Considering the fact that the appeal is limited to the finding regarding
contributory negligence and that the quantum of compensation awarded to the
petitioners is not under challenge, we have failed to comprehend why the award
3 insurance company
4 First Appeal No. 194 of 2026

--- Page 254 ---
3
was not directed to be satisfied by the insurance company pending further
orders in the appeal. The approach of the High Court does not appear to be
justified.
7. We could have set aside paragraph 4 of the impugned order and remitted
the matter to the High Court for fresh consideration; however, we consider it
expedient  to  decide  this  special  leave  petition  upon  hearing  the  insurance
company. Since we propose to issue notice, our observations as above are to be
treated as tentative.
8. Issue notice, returnable on 3rd August, 2026.
9.  Dasti, in addition, is permitted.
10. The insurance company may file an affidavit within ten days of receipt of
notice showing cause why paragraph 4 of the impugned order should not be set
aside.
(JATINDER KAUR)                             (SUDHIR KUMAR SHARMA)
P.S. to REGISTRAR                             COURT MASTER (NSH)

--- Page 255 ---

ARBITRATION PETITION NO.44/2026
BROADBAND TELECOM, INC. PETITIONER(S)
                              VERSUS
KUFRI HOTELS PRIVATE LIMITED RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. This petition has been filed under Section 11(6)(a)
read  with  Section  11(12)(a)  of  the  Arbitration  and
Conciliation Act, 1996 (Act), for the appointment of a
Sole Arbitrator.
2. The  petitioner  is  a  company  that  has  been
incorporated in California, USA, whereas the respondent
is an Indian company based in Ahmedabad. It seems that
both parties had initially suggested their respective
arbitrators,  but  the  suggestions  were  mutually
unacceptable.
3. The petitioner had thus approached this Court, due to
the prevailing difference of opinion with respect to the
choice of the Arbitrator.
4. Upon  notice  being  issued,  the  learned  Senior
Counsel/counsel for the parties have jointly submitted
that Hon’ble Mr. Justice A.K. Sikri, a Former Judge of
this Court, be appointed as the sole Arbitrator. Ordered
accordingly.
5. The parties are directed to contact the office of
Hon’ble Mr. Justice A.K. Sikri for initiation of the
formal proceedings. Since the respondent has not filed
any counter affidavit, it is clarified that all the
objections  raised  on  its  behalf  are  kept  open  and
permitted to be raised before the learned Arbitrator.
6. The  learned  Arbitrator  shall  affix  his
1

--- Page 256 ---
remuneration/fees keeping in view the Fourth Schedule of
the Act.
7. The petition is, accordingly, disposed of.

[Arising out of impugned judgment and order dated  29-04-2026 in

PRATAP SINGH BHAKHAR SINGH RAJPUT                  Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF GUJARAT                                   Respondent(s)
IA No. 153577/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED
JUDGMENT, IA No. 153579/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 20-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARUN PALLI
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Siddhant Sharma, AOR
                   Mr. Maulik Soni, Adv.
                   Mr. Vikram Choudhary, Adv. 
                  
                   
For Respondent(s)  Ms. Aneesha Rastogi, Adv.
                   Ms. Swati Ghildiyal, AOR
                   Ms. Apoorva Anand, Adv.   
Mr. Adarsh Dubey, Adv.*    
            
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard  Mr.  Siddhant  Sharma,  learned  counsel  for  the
petitioner and  Mr. Adarsh Dubey,  learned counsel for the
State.
2. This matter calls the correctness of the order  dated

--- Page 262 ---
2
29.04.2026 in R/CRMA (for Anticipatory Bail) No. 9330/2026
passed by the High Court of Gujarat at Ahmedabad. By the said
order the prayer for anticipatory bail of the petitioner is
rejected by the High Court.
3. When this matter came up on 19.05.2026, while issuing
notice, we made an order granting interim protection to the
petitioner. It is not disputed that after the interim order,
the petitioner had appeared before the concerned Investigating
Officer. The charge sheet is yet to be filed. We have
considered the relevant records. The petitioner has been
implicated based on a confession of a co-accused. There are no
criminal antecedents.
4. In the facts and circumstances of the case, we confirm
the interim order granted to the petitioner by this Court on
19.05.2026 and direct as follows-
“In the event of arrest, the petitioner shall be
released  on  anticipatory  bail  subject  to  the
satisfaction of the concerned Investigating Officer in
connection with FIR being C.R.No.11192017250310/2025
registered  with  Detroj  Police  Station,  Ahmedabad
District, Gujarat, for the offences punishable under
Sections  65(a),  65(e),  116-B,  98(2)  &  81  of  the
Gujarat Prohibition Act, 1949. The petitioner shall,
however,  continue  to  cooperate  with  the
investigations.”
5. With the above observations, the Special Leave Petition
is disposed of.

--- Page 263 ---
3
6. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed
of.
(NIRMALA NEGI)                         (MANOJ KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                COURT MASTER (NSH)  
*appearance not given

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION(S)(CIVIL)  NO(S).  3312/2025
PREETI GAUR                                   PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
MOHIT SHARMA                                  RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. This petition has been filed by the petitioner-wife
seeking transfer of HMA No. 573/2024 titled as "Mohit Sharma
vs. Preeti Gaur”, pending before the Family Court, Ambala City,
Haryana to the Family Court, District Shahdara, Karkardooma
Courts, Delhi.
2. By this Court’s order dated 24.03.2026, the matter was
referred  for  mediation  and  as  per  office  report  dated
13.07.2026 mediation report is not received. However, learned
counsel for the parties informed at the Bar that mediation has
failed.
3. Having heard learned counsel appearing for the parties,
we  are  inclined  to  transfer  the  case  sought  for.  We,
accordingly, allow the transfer petition and direct that the
HMA No. 573/2024 titled as  "Mohit Sharma vs. Preeti Gaur ”,
pending before the Family Court, Ambala City, Haryana, be
transferred to the Family Court, District Shahdara, Karkardooma
Courts, Delhi.
4. However, if the video conferencing facility is available
with the Court, the benefit of the same shall be extended to
the parties, unless their physical presence is required.

--- Page 280 ---
2
5. The transferor Court shall forthwith transmit the record
of the aforesaid case to the transferee court.
6. The transferee Court shall make all possible endeavours
first for an amicable settlement between the parties, failing
which, for early decision in the matter without unnecessary
adjournments.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of. 
………………………………………………………….J.
[K.V. VISWANATHAN]
 ……………………………………………………...J.
    [ARUN PALLI]

be given benefit of video conferencing.

1. This petition has been filed by the petitioner-wife
seeking transfer of FCOP No. 129/2025 titled as  "K.Kishore
Kumar Vs. A. Navya @ Navyashree ”, pending before the Family
Court, Vellore, Tamil Nadu to the Family Court, Bengaluru,
Karnataka.
2. None appears for the respondent in spite of service of
notice.
3. At the outset, learned counsel for the petitioner submits
that there is an error in the petition itself and also in the
order passed by this Court on 17.03.2026 in paragraph 4
inasmuch as the underlying proceeding FCOP No.189/2025 has been
wrongly mentioned as FCOP No.129/2025. 
4. The order of 17.03.2026 is hereby corrected and be read
as under:-
“4. In the meantime, there shall be stay of further
proceedings in FCOP No.189/2025, titled as “K. Kishore
Kumar Vs. A. Navya @Navyashree”, pending before the
Family Court, Vellore, Tamil Nadu.”
5. Having  heard  lea rned  counsel  appearing  for  the
petitioner, we are inclined to transfer the case sought for.
We, accordingly, allow the transfer petition and direct that
the FCOP No.189/2025 titled as "K.Kishore Kumar Vs. A. Navya @

--- Page 292 ---
2
Navyashree”, pending before the Family Court, Vellore, Tamil
Nadu be transferred to the Family Court, Bengaluru, Karnataka.
6. However, if the video conferencing facility is available
with the Court, the benefit of the same shall be extended to
the parties, unless their physical presence is required.
7. The transferor Court shall forthwith transmit the record
of the aforesaid case to the transferee court.
8. The transferee Court shall make all possible endeavours
first for an amicable settlement between the parties, failing
which, for early decision in the matter without unnecessary
adjournments.

Upon hearing the counsel the Court made the
following
O R D E R
IA No.241520/2025:
1. The present application has been filed by the
appellant  –  Suresh  Khunte  for  suspension  of
sentence and grant of interim bail.
2. We have heard learned counsel appearing for
both sides and perused the material available on
record.
3. Considering the totality of circumstances and
also the fact that the appellant has undergone
sentence for more than six and a half (6.5) years
(approximate), we suspend the sentence imposed by
the Trial Court in connection with Sessions Trial
No.40 of 2018 and release the appellant on interim
bail on the appropriate terms and conditions as
may  be  fixed  by  the  Trial  Court,  pending
consideration  of  the  proceedings  before  this
Crl.A. @ SLP (Crl.) Nos.2414/2025           2

--- Page 299 ---
Court.  Ordered  accordingly.  However,  in  the
attending  facts,  one  of  the  conditions  shall
necessarily be that if the appellant possesses a
passport, the same shall be deposited before the
Competent Authority/Court, till the conclusion of
the Trial or directed otherwise.
4. To comply with this order, the appellant
shall  be  produced  before  the  Trial  Court
forthwith.
5. The appellant shall not misuse the liberty in
any manner and would maintain good conduct.
6. Accordingly,  the  present  interlocutory
application is allowed.
7. Hearing in the appeal is expedited.
(D. NAVEEN)                      (ANU BHALLA)

                             O R D E R
Heard  learned  Additional  Solicitor  General/senior
counsel/counsel for the parties.
2. This  case  is  a  classic  example  of  how  an  innocuous
communication  gap  and  misconception  can  lead  to  serious  and
unfortunate consequences.
3. We are not elaborating the fact in view of the sensitiveness
of the matter.
4. However, today, we find that there was a communication gap
initially and thereafter, things took an unfortunate turn.
5. Now, as all misgivings have been sorted out and the petitioner
is absolved of all penal proceedings. However, the respondent no.2-
Mr. Sanjeev Tyagi, Deputy Inspector General of Police, Bijnor,
Uttar Pradesh, who is present in Court, though not called for, has
also been appropriately sensitized about his responsibility as a
senior officer more so, with regard to the conduct expected of him

--- Page 308 ---
3
as it relates to public confidence in the office, which he may be
holding.
6. Mr. Sanjeev Tyagi, the then Superintendent of Police, Bijnor,
Uttar Pradesh, who is now promoted to the post of Deputy Inspector
General of Police, Basti Range, Uttar Pradesh, has filed a fresh
affidavit in Court today. The same is taken on record.
7. In the aforesaid background and taking a holistic and overall
view in the matter, we deem it appropriate to give a quietus to the
entire issue.
8. Accordingly, the present and all incidental proceedings, if
any, stand closed.
9. The Special Leave Petition stands disposed of.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.

for the respondent no.3.

has  recently  been  entrusted  with  the  brief  by  the
petitioner no.1 and thus, prays for time to verify the
actual position.

ancillary/consequential issues arising out of the main
issue which is involved in the present case, the Court
would  not  interfere  with  the  same  in  the  present
proceedings  as  it  would  have  to  be  dealt  with  in

REVIEW PETITION (C) No.           /2026

VENKAT MANOJ KUMAR M   PETITIONER
VERSUS
 GADDE SAI SINDHUJA       RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. Respondent  (in-person)  in  Transfer  Petition  (C)
No.2115/2025 seeks review of the order dated 09 th April, 2026,
on the grounds urged in the review petition.
2. Delay condoned.
3. Application  seeking  permission  to  appear  and  argue  in
person is rejected.
4. We have perused the review petition and considered the
grounds urged in support of the prayer for review.
5. Apart from one typographical error in paragraph 5 of the
order dated 09 th April, 2026, there is no error apparent on the
face of the record. Respondent, at the time of hearing, did not
submit that he was not prepared to argue. In fact, the order of
disposal of the transfer petition was passed, inter alia, upon due
1

--- Page 313 ---
consideration of his oral objections. We, therefore, find no good
ground for review and hold that no case for review has been set
up. 
6. Since, however, a typographical error in paragraph 5 of the
order  dated  09th April,  2026  passed  in  Transfer  Petition  (C)
No.2115/2025 has been pointed out by the respondent-applicant,
we clarify that reference to “Section 13” be read as “Section 12”
of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955. Ordered accordingly.
7. The review petition is, therefore, dismissed.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

O R D E R
1. These are the petitions filed under Section 25 of the Code
of Civil Procedure, 1908. Petitioner has filed these Transfer
Petitions seeking the following reliefs:
“Transfer the proceedings bearing Family Suit No.
167/2025, titled as “Virat Soni vs. Divya Soni”,
pending before the Family Court, Udaipur, Rajasthan
to the Family Court, Indore, Madhya Pradesh”.
And
“Transfer the proceedings bearing Family Suit No.
166/2025, titled as “Virat Soni vs. Divya Soni”,
pending before the Family Court, Udaipur, Rajasthan
to the Family Court, Indore, Madhya Pradesh”.
2.  As per the office report, notice sent to the respondent
was refused by the addressee. Accordingly, respondent is deemed to
have been served. The respondent is proceeded ex-parte. 
3. In the result, the Transfer Petitions are allowed and
1

--- Page 328 ---
Family Suit No. 167/2025 titled as “Virat Soni vs. Divya Soni”
pending before the Family Court, Udaipur, Rajasthan and Family Suit
No. 166/2025, titled as “Virat Soni vs. Divya Soni”, pending before
the Family Court, Udaipur, Rajasthan are hereby transferred to the
Family Court, Indore, Madhya Pradesh.
4. Registry to communicate this order to the transferor court
for taking steps in accordance with this order.
5. On an application being made by the respondent, the Court
to which the case is transferred shall permit the respondent to
appear through video-conference on all dates except on the dates on
which his personal presence is absolutely mandatory.
6. The transfer petitions stand allowed in the aforesaid
terms.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
PETITION FOR SPECIAL LEAVE TO APPEAL (CRL.) NO.11324/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 20-05-2026
in  CRMABAU482BNSS  No.3539/2026  passed  by  the  High  Court  of
Judicature at Allahabad]
RAVIRAJ KUSHWAHA                                   PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF U.P.                                      RESPONDENT(S)
FOR ADMISSION 
IA No. 179301/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 20-07-2026 This matter was called for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANMOHAN 
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Shivam Raghuwanshi, Adv.
                   Mr. Ram Ji Dwivedi, Adv.
                   Ms. Isha Nagpal, Adv.
                   Ms. Aarjav Rajvi, Adv.
                   Mr. Pragya Sharma, Adv.
                   Mr. Sriram P., AoR                   
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Arup Banerjee, AoR
                   Mr. R.K.Srivastava, Adv.
                   Mr. Kumar Rupak, Adv.
                   Mr. Sanjeev Sharma, Adv.
                   Mr. Prakash Sharma, Adv.
                   Mr. Shiv Pratap Singh, Adv.
                   Mrs. Noopur Dubey, Adv.
                   Mr. Rajiv Agnihotri, Adv.
                   Mr. Upender Mishra., Adv.
1

--- Page 346 ---
SLP (CRL.) NO.11324/2026
                                    
                             O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the parties. 
2. The petitioner is aggrieved by the order impugned by
which his prayer for grant of anticipatory bail has been
rejected by the High Court.
3. Learned counsel for the petitioner submitted that
initially  the  High  Court  has  granted  him  protection
subject to him cooperating with the investigation and he
has fully cooperated with the same, which has led to a
chargesheet being submitted against other accused and the
petitioner. It was submitted that three persons were made
accused including the petitioner and two of them are
enjoying  the  privilege  of  anticipatory  bail.  It  was
submitted that in the present case, even the basis of
petitioner’s implication which is a so called suicide
note, the same only indicates that he was present with the
other  two  accused  and  no  specific  role/overt  act  is
alleged against him.
4. Learned counsel for the State submits that a person
has committed suicide due to non-return of Rs.40,00,000/-
(Rupees Forty Lakhs) taken by the accused. However, on a
specific query to learned counsel for the State as to
2

--- Page 347 ---
SLP (CRL.) NO.11324/2026
whether  chargesheet  has  been  submitted  after  full
cooperation from the petitioner, the same is not denied.
5. Having considered the matter, we are inclined to allow
the  prayer  for  grant  of  anticipatory  bail  to  the
petitioner.
6. Accordingly, in the event of arrest or surrender by
the petitioner before the Trial Court within two weeks
from today, he shall be released on bail in connection
with FIR No.0284, dated 23.07.2023, registered at Police
Station Kotwali, District Jhansi, Uttar Pradesh, subject
to the conditions as may be imposed by the Trial Court and
subject to his cooperation in the trial. 
7. We further indicate that the parties would ensure that
there is no delay in the trial and the Trial Court shall
also make efforts to ensure that the trial is concluded
expeditiously.
8. The Special Leave Petition stands disposed of in the
aforementioned terms.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of. 
 (POOJA SHARMA)                                (ANJALI PANWAR)
    AR-CUM-PS                               ASSISTANT REGISTRAR
3

--- Page 348 ---

ITEM NO.32               COURT NO.12               SECTION IV-D
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (C) No(s).25804/2025
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 13-08-2025
in LPA No.2368/2025 passed by the High Court of Punjab & Haryana at
Chandigarh]
JASMINE                                            Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF HARYANA & ANR.                            Respondent(s)
IA No. 224699/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
IA No. 225650/2025 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL 
DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
IA No. 224700/2025 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL 
DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
IA No. 41213/2026 - PERMISSION TO FILE APPLICATION FOR DIRECTION
IA No. 41215/2026 - PERMISSION TO PLACE ON RECORD SUBSEQUENT FACTS 
Date : 17-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARAVIND KUMAR
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VIPUL M. PANCHOLI
For Petitioner(s) : Ms. Anitha Shenoy, Sr. Adv.
                   Ms. Pariksha, AOR
                   Ms. Kavana Rao, Adv.
                                      
For Respondent(s) : Dr. Hemant Gupta, A.A.G.
                   Mr. Akshay Amritanshu, AOR
                   Mr. Varun Goel, Adv.
                   Ms. Payal Gupta, Adv.
                   Mr. Rony John, Adv.
                   Mr. Sarthak Srivastava, Adv.
                                      
                   Mr. D. S. Chauhan, AOR
                   Ms. Ruchi Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Shikher Badial, Adv.                       
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard Ms. Anitha Shenoy, learned senior Counsel appearing for
the petitioner.
2. Respondent No.2 – Haryana Public Service Commission by an
advertisement  dated  02.08.2024  called  for  applications  for
appointment of 2,424 posts under various categories. Petitioner,
1

--- Page 355 ---
who is domiciled resident of Haryana with master's degree in
Geography, applied for the post of Assistant Professor (Geography)
vide  application  dated  19.08.2024.  Haryana  Public  Service
Commission announced the examination schedule and screening test
was fixed for 08.06.2025, and subject knowledge test scheduled to
be held on 17.08.2025. Admit card was issued to the petitioner for
Geography screening test, in which she participated. On publication
of the key answers and inviting objections from the candidates,
petitioner  filed  seven  objections  against  the  questions  and,
thereafter, the Haryana Public Service Commission uploaded the
final key answers and, thereafter, declared screening test results.
3. Being aggrieved by the same, the petitioner approached the
High Court in W.P. No.20359/2024 and sought for quashing and
declaration of the answers / questions. It was contended that the
Haryana  Public  Service  Commission  arbitrarily  deleted  valid
questions, wrongful marking and had compromised the fairness and
there was no transparency and equal opportunity, amongst other
grounds. The said Writ Petition came to be dismissed, vide order
dated  30.07.2025  (Annexure-P/3).  A  similar  petition  by  other
candidates had been filed in CWP No.22526 of 2025, whereunder the
candidates  therein  were  permitted  provisional  appearance  for
subject knowledge test. However, it was ordered that the results
not to be declared without leave of the Court. Petitioner who
pursued  her  remedy  by  filing  an  LPA  No.2368/2025  was  not
successful, or in other words, the Letters Patent Appeal came to be
dismissed vide order dated 07.08.2025. Further, in yet another Writ
Petition, i.e., W.P. No.23239/2025, the jurisdictional High Court
permitted provisional appearance in subject knowledge test and
2

--- Page 356 ---
results were ordered to be withheld. The Haryana Public Service
Commission issued a notification on 13.08.2025 postponing subject
knowledge  test  from  17.08.2025  to  13.09.2025  due  to  160
provisionally allowed selected candidates had complained about lack
of sufficient spare booklets. Subsequently, the Haryana Public
Service Commission announced the subject knowledge test would be
held on 13.09.2025. Being aggrieved by the dismissal of the Letters
Patent Appeal being LPA No.2368/2025, referred to herein supra, the
appellant had approached this Court, and while issuing notice on
10.09.2025, we permitted the petitioner to provisionally appear in
the subject knowledge test scheduled to be held on 13.09.2025 for
the post of Assistant Professor (Geography).
4. In view of this Court having directed that the results of the
petitioner should not be declared without the leave of this Court,
accordingly, the learned standing counsel appearing for the Haryana
Public Service Commission has made available the result of the
petitioner in a sealed cover. The same has been opened and found
that the petitioner is not eligible, or in other words, has failed
in the subject knowledge test. In that view of the matter, nothing
further survives for consideration in this petition. Accordingly,
the Special Leave Petition stands dismissed. Respondent No.2 –
Haryana Public Service Commission is at liberty to publish or
declare the result of the petitioner in the subject knowledge test
as per the advertisement and/or as per the extant rules governing
the same.
  (NEHA GUPTA)                                   (AVGV RAMU)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)
3

--- Page 357 ---
SLP (CRL.) NO.11334/2025

[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 24-07-2025
in CRLMC No.4922/2025 passed by the High Court of Delhi at New
Delhi]
POONAM                                             PETITIONER(S)

RAJ KUMAR & ORS.                                   RESPONDENT(S)

IA No. 181394/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
IA No. 181392/2025 - PERMISSION TO APPEAR AND ARGUE IN PERSON
IA No. 25320/2026 - RECALLING THE COURTS ORDER
 
Date : 17-07-2026 This matter was called for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K. VINOD CHANDRAN
AMICUS CURIAE       Ms. Vanshaja Shukla, Adv. 
                    Mr. Abhinav Kathuria, Adv. 
For Petitioner(s) : Petitioner-in-person
                    
For Respondent(s) : Ms. Ishmeet Kaur, Adv.
                    Mr. Manoj Kumar Sahu, AoR (N/P)
                   
                             O R D E R
Heard learned counsels for the parties.
2. The petitioner, duly identified by learned Amicus
Curiae and the Respondent no.3 (Suraj Pal), husband of the
petitioner, duly identified by his counsel, are present
1

--- Page 358 ---
SLP (CRL.) NO.11334/2025
in-person.
3. By our order dated 12.02.2026, we had laid down a
scheme for settlement, which was arrived at between the
parties, which required the respondent no.3 to transfer
the flat1 in question in favour of the petitioner and their
three children. Further, he was required to pay a sum of
Rs.30,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty Lakhs) in installments, as
has been indicated in Paragraph ‘2’ of the said order.
4. Today, in Court the original registered deed of the
flat in question in favour of the petitioner and her three
children has been handed over to the petitioner. On going
through it, at first glance, she says that it appears to
be in order.
5. Coming  to  the  payment  schedule,  we  find  that
Rs.10,00,000 (Rupees Ten Lakhs) as per the schedule has
already  been  paid  by  the  respondent  no.3  to  the
petitioner. With regard to the remaining Rs.20,00,000/-
(Rupees  Twenty  Lakhs),  the  same  has  to  be  paid  in
tranches, which will finally culminate in September, 2027.
6. At this stage, learned counsel for the respondent
no.3, on instructions, submits that the said schedule
would  he  adhered  to.  We  have  also  personally  sought
response from respondent no.3 and he has assured and
undertaken that the schedule for such payment will not be
1   A-121, Third floor with roof/terrace rights, Sharda Puri, New Delhi – 110015.
2

--- Page 359 ---
SLP (CRL.) NO.11334/2025
defaulted.
7. At  this  juncture,  the  parties  agreed  that
consideration of the prayer for granting them divorce by
mutual consent by exercising our power under Article 142
of the Constitution of India, be made after payment has
been made, is accepted. Accordingly, list on 13.10.2027 at
3.50 p.m.
8. We also give liberty to the parties that in case some
urgent issue arises with regard to the present matter,
requiring  attention  of  this  Court  or  passing  of  any
further orders, it shall be open to the parties to move an
appropriate application in the present petition for taking
up the matter prior to the next date fixed.
9. We reiterate that all pending inter se proceedings,
both Civil and Criminal, between the parties shall remain
stayed.
10. The parties are also cautioned not to proceed with
those matters or precipitate any civil/criminal matters
against the other side and the children. 
 (POOJA SHARMA)                                (ANJALI PANWAR)
    AR-CUM-PS                               ASSISTANT REGISTRAR
